title: Bell Labs Invents the Transistor
shortDescription: John Bardeen, Walter Brattain, and William Shockley at Bell Labs demonstrate the first point-contact transistor, replacing bulky vacuum tubes.

title: Fairchild Semiconductor Founded
shortDescription: Eight researchers leave William Shockley's lab and, backed by Fairchild Camera and Instrument, launch Fairchild Semiconductor — a company later associated with Intel, AMD, and many Silicon Valley startups.

title: Kilby Demonstrates the Integrated Circuit
shortDescription: Jack Kilby at Texas Instruments builds the first working integrated circuit by connecting transistors, resistors, and capacitors on a single germanium chip.

title: Noyce's Planar Integrated Circuit
shortDescription: Robert Noyce at Fairchild develops the planar process, enabling mass production of ICs with interconnected layers — the foundation of modern chip fabrication.

title: Moore's Law Published
shortDescription: Gordon Moore observes that transistor counts on chips double roughly every year — a pace he would revise to every two years in 1975. The prediction becomes the industry's roadmap and a trillion-dollar economic force.

title: Intel Founded
shortDescription: Robert Noyce and Gordon Moore leave Fairchild to found Intel. The company would dominate microprocessors and define the PC era.

title: Intel 4004 — First Commercial Microprocessor
shortDescription: Intel releases the 4004, a 4-bit CPU on a single chip. It launches the microprocessor industry and the era of programmable computing.

title: Japan Launches VLSI Research Program
shortDescription: Japan's MITI coordinates a national VLSI project pooling five electronics giants — NEC, Hitachi, Fujitsu, Mitsubishi, and Toshiba — to close the gap with American chipmakers.

title: Japan Overtakes US in DRAM
shortDescription: Japanese firms capture over 70% of the global DRAM market. NEC, Hitachi, and Fujitsu undercut American rivals with higher quality and lower prices.

title: US Files Semiconductor Trade Complaint Against Japan
shortDescription: The US Semiconductor Industry Association files a Section 301 complaint alleging below-cost pricing by Japanese chipmakers and limited market access for American firms in Japan.

title: TSMC Founded
shortDescription: Morris Chang launches Taiwan Semiconductor with government backing, establishing the pure-play foundry model of manufacturing chips designed by other companies.

title: ASML Founded
shortDescription: Philips and ASM International launch ASM Lithography, later ASML, as a joint venture in the Netherlands. The company would become the world's sole supplier of extreme ultraviolet lithography machines.

title: SEMATECH Consortium Launched
shortDescription: The US government and 14 chipmakers form SEMATECH to coordinate R&D and restore American manufacturing competitiveness against Japan.

title: US-Japan Semiconductor Trade Agreement
shortDescription: Washington and Tokyo sign the first major government-brokered chip trade deal, setting floor prices on Japanese chips and pushing for greater foreign market share in Japan — a target made explicit at 20% in the 1991 renewal.

title: Intel Pentium Launched
shortDescription: Intel ships the Pentium, its fifth-generation x86 processor, strengthening the company's position in the PC processor market.

title: Samsung Becomes World's Top DRAM Maker
shortDescription: A decade after entering the memory business, Samsung overtakes its Japanese rivals to become the world's largest DRAM producer — a position it has never relinquished.

title: Dot-Com Bust Reduces Semiconductor Capex
shortDescription: After years of fab expansion during the internet boom, the dot-com downturn reduces semiconductor demand. Memory and logic makers cut capital spending, and several firms merge or exit the market in the years that follow.

title: Intel and HP Launch Itanium
shortDescription: Intel and HP introduce Itanium, a 64-bit server architecture intended for high-end systems. High cost and limited software compatibility slow adoption, while x86 and ARM-based designs continue to expand in servers and other markets.

title: ASML Takes the Lead on EUV
shortDescription: Building on 1990s research from the US national labs' EUV LLC consortium, ASML commits to commercializing extreme ultraviolet lithography — a bet requiring two decades and billions of dollars, but essential for sub-7nm chips.

title: iPhone Launches with ARM-Based Processor
shortDescription: Apple ships the iPhone with a Samsung-fabricated ARM-based processor. Growth in smartphones increases demand for mobile chips and contributes to wider use of ARM designs and foundry capacity in Asia.

title: China National IC Industry Investment Fund
shortDescription: Beijing establishes the state-backed 'Big Fund' with over $20 billion to reduce dependence on foreign semiconductors, seeding China's push for chip self-sufficiency.

title: Made in China 2025 Announced
shortDescription: China unveils an industrial plan targeting 70% self-sufficiency in core components and materials, including chips, by 2025. The plan draws scrutiny from policymakers in the United States and allied countries.

title: Huawei Added to US Entity List
shortDescription: The US Commerce Department adds Huawei to the Entity List, restricting US firms from supplying the company without a license.

title: TSMC 5nm Mass Production
shortDescription: TSMC begins high-volume production of 5nm chips for Apple and other customers at its contract foundries.

title: US Cuts Huawei Off from TSMC
shortDescription: New US export rules restrict TSMC from fabricating chips for Huawei when using American equipment, limiting Huawei's access to advanced processors made offshore.

title: US CHIPS and Science Act Signed
shortDescription: Congress allocates $52 billion to subsidize domestic chip manufacturing and R&D, the largest industrial policy investment in US semiconductor history.

title: Netherlands Restricts ASML's DUV Exports to China
shortDescription: The Netherlands restricts ASML from exporting certain advanced deep ultraviolet (DUV) immersion lithography tools to China. EUV systems had already been subject to export licensing requirements for China.

title: EU Chips Act Enters Force
shortDescription: The European Union commits €43 billion to double its global chip production share to 20% by 2030, aiming to reduce reliance on Asian fabs.

title: AI Chip Export Controls Tightened
shortDescription: The US expands October 2023 restrictions on advanced AI chips such as NVIDIA's H100, tightening license requirements for sales to China and certain other destinations.

title: TSMC Arizona Fab Operational
shortDescription: TSMC reports volume production of 4nm chips at its first advanced fab in Phoenix, Arizona, supported in part by CHIPS Act subsidies.
